---
title: "GAC Model Exploration"
format: html
---

```{r}
sheet_names <- readxl::excel_sheets("wbs_models/workbooks/granular-activated-carbon-gac.xlsm")

# Loop through each sheet and extract first column of first 3 rows
sheet_contents <- lapply(sheet_names, function(sheet) {
  df <- readxl::read_excel("wbs_models/workbooks/granular-activated-carbon-gac.xlsm", 
                           sheet = sheet,
                           range = "A1:A3")  # Only first column
  
  df |> tidyr::pivot_longer(tidyr::everything())
}) |> 
  dplyr::bind_rows()


colnames(sheet_contents) <- c("sheet_name", "content")

sheet_contents <- sheet_contents |> dplyr::filter(!is.na(content))

```

```{python}
import os
import pandas as pd
from oletools.olevba import VBA_Parser

file_path = os.path.expanduser("wbs_models/workbooks/granular-activated-carbon-gac.xlsm")
vbaparser = VBA_Parser(file_path)

# The extract_macros() method returns a tuple for each VBA module found:
# filename: The original file name
# stream_path: The internal path within the Office file structure
# vba_filename: The name of the VBA module (like "Sheet1", "ThisWorkbook", "Module1")
# vba_code: The actual VBA source code as a string

for (filename, stream_path, vba_filename, vba_code) in vbaparser.extract_macros():
    print(f"Module: {vba_filename}")
    print(vba_code)
    print("-" * 80)


modules = []
for (filename, stream_path, vba_filename, vba_code) in vbaparser.extract_macros():
    modules.append({
        'filename': filename,
        'stream_path': stream_path,
        'module_name': vba_filename,
        'code': vba_code,
        'line_count': len(vba_code.split('\n'))
    })

df = pd.DataFrame(modules)
df.to_csv("wbs_models/analysis/outputs/vba_modules.csv", index=False)
print("Saved to vba_modules.csv")
print(f"\nFound {len(modules)} modules:")
print(df[['module_name', 'line_count']])
```
